1: “After the plague” — the second census begins in the aftermath of the Baal Peor disaster, which killed 24,000 and effectively ended the first generation. The commissioning of the census to “Moses and Eleazar son of Aaron the priest” is significant: Aaron died in Num 20:28; Eleazar has now assumed the high-priestly role, and the second census is the first major act under the new priestly leadership. The pairing of Moses (dying first generation) with Eleazar (continuing into Canaan) mirrors the transition the census itself records.
2: The census criteria are identical to ch 1: “all males twenty years old and upward, by their ancestral houses, all in Israel who are able to go to war.” The military-service framing (kol yotsei tzava, all who go out to war) confirms the census is a fighting roster for the conquest ahead, not merely a population count. But the census is simultaneously an inheritance register: v52-56 will use these numbers to allocate land proportionally. The second census thus serves two purposes — military readiness and covenant land-distribution.
3: The location — “plains of Moab beside the Jordan across from Jericho” — places this census at the threshold of the promised land. The first census (ch 1) was taken at Sinai, the mountain of covenant-formation; the second census is taken at the Jordan's edge, the moment before entry. The progression from Sinai to Moab marks the entire wilderness generation's journey in a single geographical move: from law-giving to land-entry.
4: “Register everyone twenty years and older, as the LORD had commanded Moses — these being the Israelites who came out of Egypt.” The phrase “who came out of Egypt” identifies the second generation's descent from the Exodus people, not their personal experience of the Exodus. The second generation was born in the wilderness (or came out as children too young for the census); they are the Israelites by lineage and covenant, heirs of the promise even though most did not personally remember Egypt. Their identity is rooted in the founding event, not in personal memory.
5: Reuben, the firstborn of Israel — the census begins with the firstborn tribe, maintaining the precedence of Reuben's birth order. The clan structure (mishpachot, families/clans) below the tribal level records each clan descended from Reuben's four sons. Reuben's count has declined from 46,500 (ch 1) to 43,730 — a loss of 2,770. The tribal genealogies embedded in the census provide the ancestral legitimacy for each clan's portion of the land.
6: Hezron and Carmi are two of Reuben's four sons (Gen 46:9). The Carmites and Hezronites are the continuing clan-lines of Reuben. The census format — from X, the Xites — is a standard genealogical-administrative listing that echoes the clan-registration (le-mishpechotam le-beit avotam) pattern used throughout Numbers.
7: Reuben total: 43,730. Reuben's decline (from 46,500 in Num 1) is modest compared to the catastrophic decline of Simeon (below). Reuben's losses in the wilderness period may include the Dathan and Abiram rebellion (Num 16), which was a Reubenite leadership crisis. Despite Reuben's primogeniture, the tribe is numerically smaller than Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun by this count — a trajectory that will see Reuben become a minor tribe in the settlement period.
8: A genealogical aside: the son of Pallu was Eliab. This single-verse note sets up the parenthetical about Dathan and Abiram in vv9-11. The census list is not merely administrative but annotated with theological commentary — the names of the faithless are embedded in the roll of the second generation as a permanent warning.
9: “The sons of Eliab: Nemuel, Dathan, and Abiram. This is the Dathan and Abiram who were prominent in the congregation — those who contended against Moses and Aaron in the company of Korah, when they contended against the LORD.” The interjection into the census list identifies the notorious rebels of Num 16 by name within the tribal genealogy. The insertion serves a memorial function: the second generation reading the census will recognize in their own tribal lists the names of the faithless first generation. The genealogy becomes a theological history embedded in bureaucratic form.
10: “The earth opened its mouth and swallowed them along with Korah when that company died — when fire devoured the two hundred fifty men, and they became a warning sign (nes, sign/banner).” Nes can mean both a warning sign and a standard/banner, suggesting the 250 men who died by fire became a permanent cautionary symbol — not just a historical event but a standing monument of the consequences of challenging YHWH's appointed order. The event is recalled within the genealogical list as a way of reading the silence: Korah's direct sons died here; only his collateral descendants continue.
11: “But the sons of Korah did not die.” This three-word sentence (uvnei-Qorach lo metu) is one of the most theologically significant interjections in the census. The Korahite clan survived the judgment and continues into the second generation. The Psalms contain a significant corpus attributed to “the Sons of Korah” (Pss 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88) — some of Israel's most profound hymns of trust and longing. The survival of Korah's sons illustrates that the sins of the fathers do not inevitably destroy the children; the covenant extends through repentance and survival.
12: The clans of Simeon are listed next. Their total (v14) will be 22,200 — a catastrophic decline from 59,300 in Num 1. This is a reduction of 37,100, or 63% of Simeon's first-generation number. No explicit explanation is given in ch 26, but the Zimri incident (Num 25:14) identified a Simeonite clan leader as the man killed at Peor, and the 24,000 who died in the plague were disproportionately Simeonite. The genealogical clan names here — Nemuel, Jamin, Jachin — record the survivors, not the fallen.
13: Zerah and Shaul complete Simeon's five clans. The clan of Shaul (son of Simeon by a Canaanite woman, Gen 46:10) is included in the census without comment — the Canaanite maternal line does not exclude Simeonites from the covenant community. The Shaulites represent the mixed-ancestry segment of the tribe that survived the wilderness generation.
14: Simeon total: 22,200 — the lowest of any tribe in the second census, and the most dramatic decline from the first. Simeon's territory in the later settlement (Josh 19:1-9) is embedded within Judah's allotment because Simeon was too small to hold an independent region. The tribe effectively disappears as a distinct entity in the settlement period (Deut 33 omits Simeon from Moses's tribal blessings), its remnant absorbed into Judah — a trajectory that begins with this census.
15: Gad's clans begin the next tribal entry. Gad was the firstborn son of Zilpah, Leah's maidservant (Gen 30:10-11). Gad's territory will be in Transjordan (Num 32), settled by agreement with Moses before the conquest. The seven clans named here (Zephon through Areli) preserve the genealogical structure of Gad's extended family at the wilderness' end.
16: Ozni and Eri — two of Gad's seven clans. The name Ozni (from ozen, ear) and Eri (my watcher) appear only here in the genealogical record. The Oznites and Erites are minor clans within Gad whose significance is their continuity from the generation that left Egypt (Gen 46:16) to the generation entering Canaan.
17: Arod and Areli complete Gad's clan list. The correspondence between the clan names here and those in Gen 46:16 (the Exodus generation) confirms the census as a genealogical register tracing continuous descent: the second generation clans are identified by their founding ancestors from the Exodus roster.
18: Gad total: 40,500 — down from 45,650 in ch 1, a decline of about 11%. Gad's decrease is proportional to the general wilderness attrition and consistent with a tribe that participated in the various rebellions without catastrophic singular events of judgment.
19: A genealogical note for Judah: “The sons of Judah: Er and Onan. But Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan.” This parenthetical references Gen 38:7-10, where YHWH killed Er and Onan for their wickedness. The census cannot enumerate clans for Er and Onan because they died without heirs; only the surviving Judahite sons (Shelah, Pharez, Zerah) generate the clans. The Canaanite-period deaths are recalled here to explain why Judah has only three active clan-lines rather than five.
20: Judah's three surviving clan-lines: Shelah (Judah's son by the Canaanite Shua's daughter, Gen 38:5), Pharez/Perez (Judah's son by Tamar, Gen 38:29), and Zerah (Tamar's twin, Gen 38:30). The Tamar connection — irregular and scandalous in origin — is included without shame in the Judahite genealogy. Matthew's genealogy of Jesus includes Tamar (Matt 1:3), Rahab, Ruth, and Bathsheba — women whose stories were irregular, all woven into the royal Davidic line.
21: The sons of Pharez (Perez) generate two further Judahite sub-clans: Hezron and Hamul (from Gen 46:12). The Hezronite line is significant: David descends from Hezron through Ram (Ruth 4:18-22; Matt 1:3-6), making the Hezronites the clan-line from which the Davidic dynasty emerges. The census records this clan at the wilderness' end; two generations later, the Hezronite Boaz will marry Ruth, and four generations after that, David will be born.
22: Judah total: 76,500 — up from 74,600 in ch 1, a growth of 1,900. Judah is the largest tribe in both censuses, consistent with the Judahite tradition of primacy that culminates in the Davidic monarchy. The tribe's growth contrasts with Simeon's collapse and reinforces the trajectory of Genesis' blessings: Jacob's blessing on Judah (“the scepter shall not depart from Judah”, Gen 49:10) is reflected in the tribe's numerical endurance and dominance.
23: Issachar's clans: from Tola, the Tolaites; from Pua, the Punites. These correspond to the sons of Issachar in Gen 46:13. Issachar was a son of Leah (Gen 30:18), the sixth of the Leah sons, and his tribe's settlement in the fertile Jezreel Valley made Issachar agriculturally prosperous in the settlement period. The census preserves the four-clan structure of the tribe from Exodus to Canaan.
24: Jashub and Shimron complete Issachar's four clans. The name Jashub (he returns) may be an alternate name for Job (cf. Gen 46:13 LXX, where Iob appears in this position). The Shimronites descend from Shimron, the fourth of Issachar's sons, a clan that will settle in the Jezreel area of lower Galilee.
25: Issachar total: 64,300 — up significantly from 54,400 in ch 1, a growth of 9,900. Issachar is among the faster-growing tribes in the second generation, which may reflect the tribe's distance from the major rebellion incidents (Korah, Peor) or simply demographic variation. In the later period, Issachar is known for its wisdom (1 Chron 12:32: “men of Issachar who had understanding of the times”).
26: Zebulun's three clans (Sered, Elon, Jahleel) match the sons of Zebulun in Gen 46:14. Zebulun was the sixth and last of Leah's sons (Gen 30:20) and will settle in the region between the Jezreel Valley and Galilee. Jacob's blessing on Zebulun (“by the shore of the sea he shall dwell”, Gen 49:13) and the Deuteronomy blessing (“rejoice, Zebulun, in your going out”, Deut 33:18) suggest a seafaring or trading role that matches the northern Galilean coastal proximity.
27: Zebulun total: 60,500 — up from 57,400 in ch 1, a modest growth of 3,100. Zebulun's continued health contrasts with the dramatic losses of the southern tribes. Matthew's Gospel notes that the region of Zebulun was the heartland of Jesus's early Galilean ministry (Matt 4:13-16), fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy about “the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali” seeing a great light (Isa 9:1-2).
28: The sons of Joseph (through his two sons Manasseh and Ephraim) are counted as two separate tribes — maintaining the twelve-tribe structure despite Levi's removal as a land-owning tribe. Jacob's adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh as his own sons (Gen 48:5) gave Joseph a double portion, consistent with the firstborn's double inheritance (Deut 21:17). The Josephite tribes will receive the central hill country of Canaan in the settlement.
29: Manasseh's genealogy descends through Machir (“Machir fathered Gilead”) to the sub-clan of the Gileadites. The Machir clan had already been active in Transjordanian territory (Num 32:39-40), where Machir's descendants conquered and settled Gilead before the main entry into Canaan. The Gileadite sub-tribe gave its name to the entire Transjordanian highland region east of the Jordan.
30: The Gileadite clans: Jeezer (= Abiezer, Josh 17:2) and Helek. Abiezer's clan will later produce Gideon (Judg 6:11, 34), one of the major judges of the settlement period. The census preserves the genealogical roots of tribes that will become major actors in the next generation's history — the census is prospective as well as retrospective.
31: Asriel, Shechem, and continuing Gileadite sub-clans. The clan of Shechem (not the city but a Manassite clan member) is recorded here with no reference to the Dinah incident — the clan persists as a legitimate Israelite lineage regardless of the complex history of Shechem as a place name in the tradition.
32: Shemida and Hepher complete the Gileadite sub-clans. The Hepherites will be significant in the next chapter: Zelophehad was “son of Hepher” (v33), and his daughters' case about female inheritance (Num 27) will generate a legal precedent for all of Israel. The census embeds in v33 the genealogical information that makes the Zelophehad case intelligible.
33: “Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons, only daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.” This sentence is the genealogical setup for Num 27:1-11, where these five daughters petition Moses for their father's inheritance. The census list is doing double duty: recording the Hepherite population and flagging the inheritance problem that requires legal resolution. The women are named in the census — a rare distinction that signals their legal significance. Their case will generate a ruling that permanently modifies Israelite inheritance law.
34: Manasseh total: 52,700 — up dramatically from 32,200 in ch 1, a growth of 20,500 (64%). Manasseh is the fastest-growing tribe in the second census, a striking contrast to Ephraim's decline (below). The reversal of primogeniture — Jacob blessed Ephraim over Manasseh (Gen 48:19) — appears to reverse in the census data: Manasseh outgrows Ephraim in the second generation. Both tribes' land allotments in ch 17 of Joshua will reflect their relative sizes.
35: Ephraim's clans: Shuthelah, Becher, and Tahan (Gen 46:20 LXX; cf. Gen 46:20 MT which has different names for Ephraim's sons — textual complexity in the Josephite genealogies). Shuthelah is the primary Ephraimite clan; the name appears also in 1 Chron 7:20-27, which traces the Ephraimite lineage down to Joshua son of Nun — confirming that Joshua descends from Ephraim's Shuthelah line.
36: The sub-clan of Eran, son of Shuthelah, rounds out the Ephraimite genealogy. The Eranite clan is not mentioned elsewhere in the OT; its preservation here in the census is the sole record of this Ephraimite sub-family's existence. The level of genealogical detail in the census — tracking not just tribal founders but their children and grandchildren — reflects a sophisticated administrative record-keeping tradition.
37: Ephraim total: 32,500 — down from 40,500 in ch 1, a decline of 8,000. Ephraim's decrease contrasts with Manasseh's dramatic growth. The combined Joseph tribes (52,700 + 32,500 = 85,200) exceed the single-tribe totals of any other tribe except Judah (76,500), confirming Jacob's prediction that Ephraim and Manasseh would be “a multitude of nations” (Gen 48:19). Despite the decline, Ephraim will emerge as the dominant northern tribe after the settlement, eventually giving its name to the Northern Kingdom.
38: Benjamin's clans begin the enumeration of the smaller Rachel-group tribes. Five clans are named: Bela, Ashbel, Ahiram, Shupham, and Hupham — descending from Benjamin's sons (Gen 46:21 lists ten sons, but the clan-list here traces five surviving lines). Benjamin's territory will be the smallest allotment in Canaan, positioned between Judah and Ephraim, but strategically placed at the center of the future kingdom. Jerusalem is on the Benjamin-Judah border.
39: The Shuphamites and Huphamites are two of Benjamin's clan-lines not individually attested outside this census. The Hupham/Huppim connection appears in 1 Chron 7:12 in a compressed form. The Benjaminite clan lists here and in 1 Chron 7-8 diverge somewhat, reflecting the tribal record's preservation across different archival traditions.
40: An embedded genealogical note: “the sons of Bela were Ard and Naaman,” generating two further Benjaminite sub-clans (the Ardites and the Naamites). Ard and Naaman appear as sons (not grandsons) of Benjamin in Gen 46:21, but here as grandsons — a genealogical compression common in OT tribal records. The Naaman connection becomes relevant in later tradition: Naaman the Syrian (2 Kings 5) bears the same name as this Benjaminite clan-founder, though no relationship is established.
41: Benjamin total: 45,600 — up from 35,400 in ch 1, a growth of 10,200. Benjamin's significant growth in the second generation is notable given the catastrophic decimation of Benjamin in the later civil war (Judg 20-21, where all but 600 Benjaminite men were killed). The relative strength of Benjamin at this point makes the later near-extinction all the more striking. Paul the apostle identifies as a Benjaminite (Phil 3:5), a tribe whose survival was itself a miraculous preservation.
42: Dan's clans: only one is named — Shuham (also spelled Hushim in Gen 46:23), generating the Shuhamites. Dan has only a single clan-line, the smallest clan-structure of any tribe. Dan's large census total despite a single genealogical line suggests that Shuham's descendants multiplied dramatically. The single-clan structure may also reflect the absorption of neighboring peoples or the loss of other Danite lines in the wilderness period.
43: Dan total: 64,400 — up from 62,700 in ch 1, a slight increase of 1,700. Dan maintains its position as one of the larger tribes, second only to Judah in this census. The Danite tendency to migrate (they eventually settle far north at Laish/Dan, Judg 18) may account for population stability through geographic flexibility rather than rootedness in a single region.
44: Asher's clans begin with Jimna, Jesui, and Beriah — three of the four sons named in Gen 46:17. Asher was the eighth son of Jacob, born to Zilpah (Gen 30:12-13). His name (happy/blessed) connects to Leah's exclamation at his birth. Asher's territory in the settlement will be the northwestern coastal strip from Carmel to Phoenicia — prosperous and strategically placed.
45: The Heberites and Malchielites descend from Beriah's two sons. These sub-clans within Asher are attested in 1 Chron 7:31-32. The level of sub-clan detail (tribe → clan → sub-clan) in the census reflects a three-tier genealogical system used for land allocation: the portion within the tribe's region was further divided by clan and sub-clan.
46: “The name of Asher's daughter was Serah.” This single sentence — the only woman named in the tribal census sections — is remarkable. Serah daughter of Asher appears in the Exodus generation census (Gen 46:17) and here in the second generation census. Later Jewish tradition (Midrash Tanchuma) credits Serah with great longevity and wisdom, making her a living link between the patriarchs and the Exodus generation. Her inclusion in both censuses (across a forty-year gap) may indicate she was still alive — one of the few first-generation survivors.
47: Asher total: 53,400 — up from 41,500 in ch 1, a growth of 11,900. Asher is among the fastest-growing tribes in the second census. Moses's blessing of Asher (Deut 33:24-25: “Asher is most blessed of sons; let him be the favorite of his brothers”) corresponds to the tribe's numerical strength and prosperity at this point. The growth trajectory confirms Asher as one of the healthier tribes entering Canaan.
48: Naphtali's clans: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem — the four sons of Naphtali (Gen 46:24). Naphtali was the sixth son of Jacob, born to Bilhah, Rachel's maidservant (Gen 30:8). His name (my wrestling) reflects Rachel's sense of competition with Leah. Naphtali's settlement in upper Galilee will place the tribe in the region Isaiah designates for the coming light (Isa 9:1-2), the area of Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matt 4:15-16).
49: Jezer and Shillem, the third and fourth Naphtalite clans. All four of Naphtali's sons have surviving clan-lines at this point, suggesting the tribe maintained a stable four-clan structure through the wilderness. The Naphtalite leader Ahira son of Enan appeared in the census (Num 1:15), the tribal offerings (Num 7:78), and the march order (Num 10:27) — a consistent administrative figure across the Numbers records.
50: Naphtali total: 45,400 — down from 53,400 in ch 1, a decline of 8,000. Naphtali is one of the declining tribes in the second census, a reversal from its strong first-generation showing. The reason for Naphtali's decline is not recorded. In the settlement period, Naphtali's territory will be contested by Canaanites and Phoenicians, consistent with a tribe that may have had more border exposure and conflict even in the wilderness period.
51: The grand total of the second census: 601,730 — down from 603,550 in ch 1, a decline of 1,820. Despite forty years in the wilderness, an entire generation's death, two major plague-and-rebellion events (Num 16 and Num 25), and innumerable hardships, Israel's military-age male population is virtually unchanged. The near-stability of the total is the census's quiet theological statement: YHWH's covenant population endures. The one generation that died was replaced by another that is ready for the conquest.
52: A new divine speech opens the land-allocation section (vv52-56). The transition from census-as-roster to census-as-inheritance-register is made explicit: “to these the land shall be divided as an inheritance” (leeileh techaleq ha-aretz leNachalah). The census numbers are not merely administrative data but the basis for covenant-land distribution. Every man counted receives a proportional share; the counting is simultaneously the measuring of the promise.
53: “To these the land shall be divided as an inheritance according to the number of names.” Berov shemot (by the multitude of names) — the number of registered individuals determines the size of each tribe's portion. The formula is fair: more people → more land; fewer people → less land. The land is distributed not by tribal history, military power, or seniority of birth but by current registered population. This principle of proportional inheritance is democratic in its application of the covenant promise.
54: “To a larger group give a larger inheritance, and to a smaller group a smaller one. Each shall receive its inheritance proportional to those numbered in it.” The principle is explicit and repeated for emphasis. The fairness of the allocation system is YHWH's design — no tribe can claim more than its registered size warrants. This prevents the larger tribes from dominating the covenant allocation and the smaller tribes from being marginalized. The proportional distribution is a covenant equity principle.
55: “Nevertheless, the land shall be divided by lot (begoral techalek et-ha-aretz).” Despite the proportional principle of vv53-54, the specific location of each tribe's portion within Canaan is determined by lot (goral). The lot is divine: Prov 16:33 states “the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH.” The combination of human calculation (proportional size) and divine determination (lot for location) means that each tribe's where is YHWH's gift, while the how much is proportional to covenant-participation. No tribe gets to claim Canaan's best land by military power or political clout.
56: “By lot the possession shall be divided, whether between a larger or smaller group.” The restatement of the lot principle affirms that it applies to all tribes equally — the lot respects no tribal precedence. Larger tribes receive more land in area, but the quality and location of the land is not manipulated to favor prominent tribes. The lot system is the covenant's mechanism for resolving the inevitable tension between different tribes' preferences and the practical geography of Canaan.
57: The Levite census follows separately (vv57-62) because the Levites receive no land inheritance — YHWH is their inheritance (Num 18:20). The Levite clan-list: Gershon, Kohath, Merari — the same three clans as the Sinai and wilderness censuses (Num 3-4). A fourth group is added: “from Gershon, the Libnites and the Hebronites and the Mahlites and the Mushites and the Korathites.”
58: The Levite sub-clans are listed more fully here: Libnite, Hebronite, Mahlite, Mushite, and Korathite. Kohath fathered Amram — the critical genealogical link to Moses, Aaron, and Miriam. The Korahites (from Izhar son of Kohath) were the clan of Korah the rebel (ch 16) whose sons survived (v11). Their inclusion in the clan list without erasure shows that genealogical record was not retroactively altered for theological reasons — the embarrassing history is preserved intact.
59: “The name of Amram's wife was Jochebed, daughter of Levi, who was born to Levi in Egypt.” Jochebed is the mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam — the three founding leaders of the Exodus generation. Her identification as “daughter of Levi” establishes her as a first-generation Levite (born in Egypt), making Moses a second-generation Levite by direct descent on both sides (Amram was also a Kohathite Levite). The matrilineal notation is unusual in OT genealogies and reflects Jochebed's historical importance as the mother of Israel's prophetic and priestly founders.
60: Aaron's four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar — the same four who were ordained at Sinai (Lev 8-9). The census records them as the Aaronic priestly family, though the continuation immediately notes the deaths of the two elder sons. Eleazar and Ithamar are the surviving lines through whom the Aaronic priesthood continues.
61: “Nadab and Abihu died when they offered unauthorized fire before the LORD.” The reference is to Lev 10:1-2, where Nadab and Abihu were consumed by divine fire for offering “strange/unauthorized fire” (esh zarah). Their deaths in the census list serve the same function as the Dathan-Abiram note in v9: the genealogical record embeds the theological warning. The priesthood's holiness-requirement is not merely a legal abstraction but a matter of life and death, as the sons of Aaron themselves discovered.
62: The Levite total: 23,000 males from a month old and upward — compared to 22,000 in Num 3:39, a growth of 1,000. The age criterion for Levites is different: starting from a month old (not twenty years), reflecting the fact that Levites are counted as a substitute for firstborns (Num 3:40-51), not as a military force. The Levites “were not enrolled among the Israelites, because there was no inheritance given to them among the Israelites.” Their separate count underlines their separate status: they are YHWH's portion, not Israel's military resource.
63: “These are those numbered by Moses and Eleazar the priest, who enrolled the Israelites in the plains of Moab beside the Jordan across from Jericho.” The location notation closes the census with a geographical marker: the Jordan and Jericho are visible from here. The second census was conducted at the threshold of the promise — the registered community is the one that will cross the river and receive the land.
64: “But not one of these was among those Moses and Aaron had registered when they counted the Israelites in the wilderness of Sinai.” The critical statement: there is zero overlap between the first census (ch 1) and the second census (ch 26), except for the two exceptions in v65. The entire registered military force of Israel has been replaced in forty years. This is the census's ultimate theological statement about Numbers' central narrative: YHWH said the first generation would die in the wilderness (Num 14:28-35), and it did. The second census is the proof of that prophecy's complete fulfillment.
65: “For the LORD had said of them: They shall surely die in the wilderness. And not one of them was left except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.” The census ends with the two exceptions to the death-decree: Caleb and Joshua — the only two of the twelve spies who brought a faithful report (Num 14:6-9, 30). Their survival is not accidental survival of strong men; it is the fulfillment of YHWH's explicit promise in Num 14:30: “not one shall enter the land, except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.” The census's final verse is Numbers' theological summary: YHWH's word of judgment was precise, complete, and exactly fulfilled; his word of mercy to the faithful was equally precise and equally fulfilled.
